behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is cross-module handoff (file discovery -> prefix parsing/sort -> execution order), not just a single pure sort helper. Real OS/network/subprocess is not required by the behavior text, and no AI/ML output is involved, so System/Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner against a temp `migrations/` directory containing out-of-order filenames (e.g., `1_*.sql`, `2_*.sql`, `10_*.sql`) with an instrumented local migration-applier/repo test double that records apply sequence; assert execution order is numeric ascending by prefix.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is state propagation across runs between migration executor and `_migrations` metadata tracking, which is beyond a single function. Higher levels were considered and rejected because behavior text only requires repeat-run idempotency, not real OS/subprocess/network edge conditions; no AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Execute runner twice against the same local test persistence layer (DB/repo fixture). After first run, rerun with identical migration files; assert previously recorded versions are skipped and migration side effects are not duplicated.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real database transactional behavior under statement failure and stop-on-error control flow. A mock/fake transaction layer can mask rollback semantics, so Integration is too low. No AI/ML dependency, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Run against a real PostgreSQL instance with a migration containing a failing statement after a prior mutating statement, followed by another migration file. Assert partial changes from the failed migration are absent (rolled back) and later migration files were not applied.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real inter-process concurrency plus DB locking/uniqueness behavior. In-process fakes or single-process tests can miss race conditions, so mocking would hide real failures. No AI/ML dependency, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes concurrently against the same real PostgreSQL database and shared migration set; after both exit, assert each migration’s side effects occurred once and `_migrations` has exactly one record per version.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Behavior targets initialization/order handoff between metadata bootstrap and migration application modules. It does not require real subprocess behavior or special OS/network effects in wording, and has no AI/ML dependency, so System/Agentic/Workflow are rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Against a fresh test DB/repo fixture, run once and capture executed operations order (or observable DB state transitions); assert `_migrations` table creation occurs before first migration file execution.
